name: raw-checksum
doc:
THE FIRST CAPABILITY CLAIM: raw Checksum exists in client-go and not in client-rust.

client-go serves rawkv.Checksum -- a SERVER-side crc64 over a key range
(kvrpcpb.RawChecksumRequest), returning crc64_xor / total_kvs / total_bytes. client-rust
has no such request type anywhere in its plan layer (rules doc RAW-4; roadmap 5.6). Its
driver answers `unsupported`, which is a first-class, comparable observation -- the
driver must NOT emulate the feature (a client-side scan-and-hash would checksum
different bytes under different semantics and manufacture agreement out of a
workaround).

So the claim surface is the CLASS: oracle `ok`, subject `unsupported`. The oracle's
checksum numbers ride along in the trace as evidence only -- crc64_xor and total_bytes
cover the key bytes, run prefix included, so they legitimately differ between the two
runs of this comparison and are never projected.

BOTH RUNS SHARE ONE SETUP: client-go writes the same three raw pairs in both; only the
CHECKER changes. Raw and transactional data are kept apart by construction (every key
lives under this run's unique prefix; no scenario mixes raw and txn ops on the same
keys; scenarios run serially) -- the APIVersion V1 caveat in kvrpcpb is about exactly
that mixing.

When client-rust gains raw checksum, this diff vanishes, ledger-check fires XCONVERGE,
and G-0002 is re-stated as an `expect = "agrees"` regression guard -- the designed
lifecycle for a closed gap.

steps.why: Seed the range so the oracle's checksum is over something: an empty-range checksum would be a weaker witness that the capability actually ran.
THE CAPABILITY CLAIM. The oracle answers `ok` (with crc64/total_kvs/total_bytes as evidence in the trace); the subject answers `unsupported`. That class difference IS G-0002.
